calendar.matters:
CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar May-20-2026 Continued To Aug-19-26 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Apr-24-2026 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: DEFENDANT TUTOR PERINI CORPORATION'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT

No appearances. The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: sustained with 15 calendar days leave to amend as to the fifth cause of action for fraud in the inducement and sustained without leave to amend as to the sixth cause of action for negligent misrepresentation.

As Abbett states in the first full paragraph of its opposition memorandum, its fifth and sixth causes of action are distinguishable from a breach of contract claim because the essence of the fifth and sixth causes of action are that TPC "never intended to perform as represented." Under California law, claims of this sort are called fraud by false promise or promissory fraud.

In the seminal case of Tenzer v. Superscope, Inc. (1985) 39 Cal. 3d 18, 30 the California Supreme Court reaffirmed the longstanding California rule that "something more than nonperformance is required to prove the defendant's intent not to perform his promise." (Quoting People v. Ashley (1954) 42 Cal. 2d 246, 263). Tenzer disapproved of the court of appeal cases that had held that the "subsequent failure to perform as promised warrants the inference that defendant did not intend to perform" the allegedly false promise. The only allegations in Abbett's complaint supporting the allegation that TPC did not intend to perform its promises are in paragraph 98. In that paragraph the only alleged facts purportedly showing TPC's intent not to perform its promises are conduct committed by TPC are alleged breaches of the TPC-Abbett contract that occurred after the promises alleged to have been falsely made. Consequently, Abbett's fifth cause of action fails to state a claim. If Abbett can do so in good faith, it is granted leave to allege ultimate facts more than contractual non-performance to show that at the time that TPC made its alleged false promises TPC had no intention to perform those promises.

Abbett's sixth cause of action for negligent misrepresentation is a claim for negligent false promise. California law does not recognize such a claim. (Tarmann v. State Farm Mutual Automobile Insurance Co. (1991) 2 Cal. App. 4th 153, 158-159).

Proposed order submitted for review. Judge: Harold E. Kahn, Clerk: E. Larnauti, Not Reported (301/HEK)
